key_strengths: 76-year heritage brand with pricing power that strengthens through deliberate scarcity
52% gross margins, 24% ROIC, and expanding profitability on flat unit volumes
Fortress balance sheet: net industrial debt EUR 32M, interest coverage 51x, trajectory to net cash by 2027
2+ year order backlog provides exceptional revenue visibility
Agnelli family ownership ensures patient long-term capital allocation
key_risks: US tariff escalation: 30%+ revenue exposure with all production in Italy and no relocation option
EV launch execution: Ferrari Luce in 2026 is a brand-defining binary event
Valuation compression: still priced at 30.7x trailing P/E with thin margin for error
Elevated capex intensity (13.2% of revenue) during EV transition may compress near-term returns
China and global luxury demand softening could pressure sentiment
red_flags: Negative margin of safety: stock trades 82% above bear-case IV
Monte Carlo shows 80% probability of overvaluation at current price
P/FCF of 67x is extreme even for a luxury compounder
2026 revenue guidance of 5% growth does not support the 10%+ CAGR needed to justify current price
buy_triggers: Stock declines to $240-270 range providing meaningful margin of safety against base-case IV
US-EU tariff resolution or reduction below 15% removing the largest overhang
Successful Ferrari Luce launch with strong order intake validating EV strategy
sell_triggers: Ferrari expands annual production above 15,000 units, dismantling the scarcity model
Brand moat demonstrably impaired through scandal, sustained F1 irrelevance, or volume dilution
Stock re-rates above 40x forward P/E without corresponding earnings acceleration
valuation_summary: Quant base-case IV of $283 vs current price of $317 (12% premium). Monte Carlo gives only 20% probability of undervaluation. At 30.7x trailing P/E, the stock has derated 39% from its 2025 peak but remains above fair value in most scenarios. Reverse-engineering $317 requires ~11% revenue CAGR with 39%+ EBITDA margins sustained for 5+ years. Third-party DCF estimates range from $150 to $510. The business quality is exceptional but the price demands near-perfect execution.
compact_checklist: Ferrari sells ~14,000 ultra-luxury sports cars per year at average prices above EUR 350k, with a 2+ year order backlog and revenue growth driven by personalization and mix — not volume — making it one of the most predictable businesses in public markets.

76 years of Formula 1 heritage and deliberate production scarcity create a self-reinforcing flywheel where waiting lists preserve resale values, which deepens aspiration and demand, producing 52% gross margins that no competitor can approach because the brand cannot be replicated with capital.

ROIC of 24.3% on rising trajectory, gross margins expanding to 52% on flat unit volumes, and EUR 1.4B in free cash flow confirm Ferrari converts pricing power into real, growing cash returns well above cost of capital.

The Agnelli family (Exor, 36.5% voting power) provides patient long-term stewardship, returning EUR 1.3B to shareholders in FY2025 via buybacks and dividends while reducing net industrial debt to EUR 32M — though programmatic buybacks at 30x earnings may not always be the best use of capital.

With net industrial debt of EUR 32M, interest coverage of 51x, and EUR 1.5B annual free cash flow, Ferrari could survive a multi-year revenue collapse without external financing and is on track for a net cash position by 2027.

At $317, the stock requires ~11% revenue CAGR with 39%+ EBITDA margins sustained for 5+ years — the quant model's Monte Carlo gives only a 20% probability of undervaluation, meaning you must believe Ferrari executes near-perfectly on the EV transition and tariff pass-through to justify today's price.

The business itself provides a thick buffer (2-year backlog, 39% EBITDA margins, near-zero debt), but there is zero price margin of safety — the stock trades 12% above base-case IV and 82% above bear-case IV, so your protection comes entirely from franchise durability, not from what you paid.
Sell if Ferrari expands annual production above 15,000 units to chase volume growth, because that would dismantle the scarcity model that underpins the entire pricing power and brand premium.
source_summary: Ferrari FY2025 annual results (Feb 2026), Ferrari Capital Markets Day 2025, Ferrari corporate filings, quant DCF model (src/quant), yfinance financial data, Yahoo Finance, analyst consensus via MarketBeat, Alpha Spread DCF, GuruFocus, Morningstar, Motley Fool, MIT Sloan case study, Bloomberg, CRIF Ratings, MacroTrends
